item.title: Britain’s prime minister is in power without purpose
item.description: Latest Mandelson scandal has left Starmer in limbo until his MPs decide his fate

item.title: Apple’s smooth CEO swap is the ultimate corporate status symbol
item.description: There are two kinds of companies — those that can do CEO transitions without intense upheaval and those that can’t

item.title: UniCredit treads fine line between activism and M&A
item.description: Italian bank’s cage-rattling is best understood as a way to break into the cage

item.title: The US cattle industry is eating its own tail
item.description: From rising temperatures to water shortages, ranchers need to be thinking about a plan B

item.title: What we need to know from Warsh
item.description: After a period of silence, Donald Trump’s nominee for Fed chair gives evidence

item.title: Why America is falling out of love with Israel
item.description: Public opinion and elite sentiment has turned decisively against Netanyahu

item.title: Europe’s AI endgame? Bet on reliability
item.description: If the region fails to lead on safe and secure AI, it risks remaining stuck on the wrong side of the tech wall

item.title: Will social media addiction go the way of cigarettes?
item.description: Smoking among the rich has declined dramatically — and digital dependency could follow a similar pattern

item.title: Tackling energy security can no longer be put off
item.description: The Middle East war should accelerate a shift to renewable power sources

item.title: Babcock’s cheaper warships offer the Royal Navy hope
item.description: The UK defence company is building Type 31 frigates quickly at Rosyth to fill an awkward gap

item.title: In the age of AI, recruiters need to rethink their jobs
item.description: The more upmarket the staffing service, the better the chances of weathering the change

item.title: William Hill’s owner can squeeze extra winnings from its suitor
item.description: A bid for owner Evoke sounds generous, but cost cuts might boost returns further

item.title: The Mandelson fiasco reveals the true nature of Starmerism
item.description: This is a prime minister who does not understand the difficult trade-offs involved in governing

item.title: Brad Jacobs’ QXO takes a high-speed approach to permanent capital
item.description: Billionaire investor’s acquisition vehicle has struck its third major building products deal in a year, spending $30bn in total

item.title: Revamping EU merger rules is not a cure-all
item.description: A bigger priority is completing the single market and streamlining corporate law

item.title: America’s coming revolt is in the ‘wired belt’
item.description: Anger in the rustbelt propelled Trump to victory — the rage of suburban knowledge workers will drive the AI backlash

item.title: The European right is pivoting away from America
item.description: Electoral logic is driving a shift as polls lay bare European disgust at the US administration

item.title: Do dogs belong in Paris?
item.description: One of Europe’s most densely populated cities has resorted to fines and exclusion zones to keep pets in check

item.title: The Iran crisis has not yet peaked
item.description: The war is currently more likely to escalate than to be resolved by negotiation

item.title: Netflix’s Reed Hastings: an icon of good leadership and bad governance
item.description: Streamer’s co-founder steps away as his progeny faces its biggest challenge

item.title: Yes, tokens do have real uses in finance
item.description: Tokenisation is taking hold in areas including money, bonds, gold, funds, real estate and private equity

item.title: The looming battle over the Fed’s balance sheet
item.description: What will Warsh choose?

item.title: How the Iran war could hurt Wall Street
item.description: The big unknown is how the conflict could alter the Gulf’s investment priorities in the next year or two

item.title: A retro rally is coming from e-merging markets
item.description: For now South Korea and Taiwan are the biggest beneficiaries of the AI wave

item.title: Out of office, still hyper-responsive
item.description: Once established by professional training, the addictive pull of screens, newsfeeds and email replies is impossible to shake
